edges.description: Loss of COG-complex integrity disrupts Golgi trafficking and enzyme compartmentalization, mislocalizing glycosylation machinery.
edges.source: COG7 Loss Destabilizes the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

edges.description: Mislocalized Golgi enzymes impair maturation of both N-linked and O-linked glycoproteins.
The combined Golgi glycosylation defect is associated with progressive microcephaly, hypotonia, growth retardation, and failure to thrive in reported COG7-CDG patients.
COG7-CDG patients can have feeding problems from gastrointestinal pseudo-obstruction, contributing to failure to thrive.
The COG7-CDG multisystem phenotype includes cardiac anomalies and episodes of extreme hyperthermia.
Wrinkled skin is reported as part of the COG7-CDG phenotype.
COG7-CDG patients can have adducted thumbs as a congenital limb manifestation.
Deficient N- and O-glycosylation produces abnormal glycoprotein glycosylation profiles.
The N-glycosylation component of the combined Golgi defect is reflected by a type II transferrin isoform profile.
Defective N-glycan processing is reflected by a type II-like sialotransferrin isoelectric focusing pattern.
edges.target: Type II-like serum transferrin isoelectric focusing pattern

edges.description: Defective O-glycosylation is reflected by increased hyposialylated apolipoprotein C-III isoforms.
Global Golgi glycosylation disruption is reflected by markedly decreased total plasma sialic acid.
The multiple Golgi glycosylation and trafficking defect is accompanied by increased plasma lysosomal enzyme activities.
Orphanet records recurrent infections as part of the multisystem COG7-CDG phenotype spectrum.
Orphanet records elevated circulating hepatic transaminases as a biochemical hepatic phenotype of COG7-CDG.
Severe multisystem COG7-CDG can include dysmorphic facial appearance as part of the congenital presentation.
Multisystem glycoprotein biosynthesis defects contribute to severe infantile liver disease.
The severe multiple Golgi glycosylation defect produced an early lethal infantile presentation in the original affected siblings.
Progressive severe microcephaly is part of the characteristic COG7-CDG phenotype.
Hypotonia is reported among characteristic neurologic findings.
Growth retardation is reported in affected COG7-CDG patients.
Failure to thrive is reported in affected COG7-CDG patients.
Orphanet records seizure as a frequent neurologic phenotype of COG7-CDG.
Orphanet records brain atrophy as a very frequent neurologic structural phenotype of COG7-CDG.
Feeding problems are reported in COG7-CDG.
Feeding problems are attributed to gastrointestinal pseudo-obstruction in the reported COG7-CDG patients.
Ventricular septal defect is named in the paper title as a consistent COG7-CDG phenotype.
Episodes of extreme hyperthermia are reported in COG7-CDG.
Wrinkled skin is represented using the HPO cutis laxa term, whose definition includes wrinkled skin.
Adducted thumbs are reported in COG7-CDG.
Severe liver disease in the reported affected siblings was associated with death within the first weeks of life.
A type II-like serum sialotransferrin pattern reports the N-glycan processing component of the combined Golgi glycosylation defect.
edges.target: Type II-like serum transferrin isoelectric focusing pattern

edges.description: Increased hyposialylated apolipoprotein C-III isoforms report the O-glycosylation component of the combined Golgi glycosylation defect.
Increased plasma lysosomal enzyme activities are a reported accompanying biochemical abnormality of the multiple Golgi glycosylation defect.
Decreased total plasma sialic acid reports defective glycoprotein sialylation in the combined Golgi glycosylation defect.

edges.target: COG7 Loss Destabilizes the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

nodes.description: Serum transferrin isoelectric focusing and apolipoprotein C-III isoelectric focusing show abnormal N-linked and O-linked glycosylation.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and biochemical presentation of siblings with COG-7 deficiency, a lethal multiple O- and N-glycosylation disorder.
evidence.snippet: Isoelectric focusing of serum sialotransferrins showed a type 2-like CDG pattern.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and biochemical presentation of siblings with COG-7 deficiency, a lethal multiple O- and N-glycosylation disorder.
evidence.snippet: O-Glycosylation was investigated by isoelectric focusing of apolipoprotein C-III, which showed increased fractions of hyposialylated isoforms.

nodes.description: Dysmorphic appearance was reported in the original affected siblings with severe infantile COG7-CDG; no more specific facial gestalt is available in the cached abstract.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and biochemical presentation of siblings with COG-7 deficiency, a lethal multiple O- and N-glycosylation disorder.
evidence.snippet: We report on two dysmorphic siblings with severe liver disease who died at the age of a few weeks.

nodes.description: Adducted thumbs are reported in affected COG7-CDG patients.

evidence.reference_title: A common mutation in the COG7 gene with a consistent phenotype including microcephaly, adducted thumbs, growth retardation, VSD and episodes of hyperthermia.
evidence.snippet: The siblings and an unrelated single child of consanguineous parents presented with growth retardation, progressive, severe microcephaly, hypotonia, adducted thumbs, feeding problems by gastrointestinal pseudo-obstruction, failure to thrive, cardiac anomalies, wrinkled skin and episodes of extreme hyperthermia.

nodes.description: Brain atrophy is recorded as a very frequent neurological phenotype of COG7-CDG in Orphanet.

evidence.snippet: HP:0012444 | Brain atrophy | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: COG7 variants impair integrity of the conserved oligomeric Golgi complex, disturbing intra-Golgi trafficking and the localization of glycosylation machinery.
nodes.id: COG7 Loss Destabilizes the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi Complex

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of the COG complex subunit gene COG7 causes a lethal congenital disorder.
evidence.snippet: The mutation impairs integrity of the COG complex and alters Golgi trafficking, resulting in disruption of multiple glycosylation pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation of the COG complex subunit gene COG7 causes a lethal congenital disorder.
evidence.snippet: Here we describe two siblings with a fatal form of CDG caused by a mutation in the gene encoding COG-7, a subunit of the conserved oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex.

nodes.description: COG7-CDG includes cardiac anomalies and episodes of extreme hyperthermia in reported patients.

evidence.reference_title: A common mutation in the COG7 gene with a consistent phenotype including microcephaly, adducted thumbs, growth retardation, VSD and episodes of hyperthermia.

nodes.description: COG7-CDG produces a type II CDG biochemical pattern with defective N-glycosylation and O-glycosylation.

evidence.reference_title: Deficiencies in subunits of the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex define a novel group of Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation.
evidence.snippet: COG defects are a novel group of CDG-II with deficient N- as well as O-glycosylation.

evidence.reference_title: A common mutation in the COG7 gene with a consistent phenotype including microcephaly, adducted thumbs, growth retardation, VSD and episodes of hyperthermia.
evidence.snippet: A combined disorder in the biosynthesis of N- and O-linked glycosylation with hyposialylation was detected.

nodes.description: Disturbed anterograde and retrograde Golgi trafficking mislocalizes glycosylation enzymes, producing defective glycoprotein maturation.

evidence.reference_title: Deficiencies in subunits of the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex define a novel group of Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation.
evidence.snippet: If the balance is disturbed, the glycosylation machinery is mislocalized, which can cause Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation type II (CDG-II), as illustrated by the identification of congenital defects in the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex in humans.

evidence.reference_title: Deficiencies in subunits of the Conserved Oligomeric Golgi (COG) complex define a novel group of Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation.
evidence.snippet: We collected findings from different COG deficient cell types, such as CHO, yeast and human fibroblasts to hypothesize about structure and function of the COG complex
